questions.prompt: When converting an M:N (many-to-many) relationship in an ER diagram into relational tables, the correct approach is to:

options.text: Merge the two participating entity sets into a single table
Add a foreign key column to either one of the existing entity tables
Create a new table whose primary key is the combination of the primary keys of the participating entities
Drop the relationship; M:N relationships cannot be represented in the relational model

questions.explanation: An M:N relationship requires its own table (often called an associative/junction table) with foreign keys referencing both entities. The combined foreign-key columns form its primary key.

questions.prompt: The relational algebra operator that returns only those tuples from R that have a match in S based on common attributes is the:
questions.explanation: The natural join combines tuples from R and S that agree on all commonly-named attributes, eliminating duplicate columns. Cartesian product just pairs everything; union/difference are set operations.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC) and Domain Relational Calculus (DRC)?

options.text: TRC variables range over tuples of relations, while DRC variables range over individual attribute (column) values
TRC is procedural while DRC is declarative
TRC supports aggregation but DRC does not allow any quantifiers
TRC is strictly less expressive than DRC

questions.explanation: Both calculi are declarative and equivalent in expressive power to safe relational algebra. The fundamental difference is the kind of variable used: TRC uses tuple variables (e.g., t.Name), DRC uses domain variables ranging over attribute values.

questions.prompt: Codd showed that relational algebra and (safe) relational calculus are:
questions.explanation: A query language is called 'relationally complete' if it can express every query expressible in relational algebra. Codd proved relational algebra and safe relational calculus are equivalent — both are relationally complete but not Turing-complete.

questions.prompt: Consider relations Student(sid, name, dept) and Enrolled(sid, course). Which relational-algebra expression returns the names of students enrolled in course 'CS101'?
questions.explanation: First select Enrolled tuples for course = 'CS101', then natural-join with Student on sid (so we associate names), and finally project the name attribute. Option A is wrong because Student does not have a course attribute.
